feed.title: SAP Community - SAP Fiori
feed.subtitle: SAP Fiori blog posts in SAP Community

entry.title: Recovering UI5 adaptation project source from a deployed system
entry.content:
Introduction

UI5 adaptations can keep running in SAP long after the original developer and their local project are gone. This post explains why you cannot treat adaptations like ordinary BSP content, how adaptation files are actually stored, and how we automated downloading them from the layered repository into a local folder. You will learn which database table holds the data, what our small ABAP report does, and what you still need to set up by hand before using the files in your IDE.

The situation

Imagine you are asked to implement additional functionality for an already extended standard UI5 application. The extension is running in the system and users rely on it. The problem is that the team never used proper source code management, and the developer who built the adaptation has left the company—along with the local project files.

So the adaptation exists in SAP and the deployment is there. In theory you only need to “download it” again. In practice this is a bit awkward: there is no single, obvious option comparable to something like /UI5/UI5_REPOSITORY_LOAD that pulls the whole project. A main reason is that these adaptation files are not stored in the BSP repository; they live in the layered repository.

Inspecting files manually

You can inspect adaptation files one by one using the Layered Repository option in transaction SUI_SUPPORT (thanks to @abdullahgunes for pointing this out). 

You can view individual files, download them manually, and rebuild the folder structure yourself. That works, but it becomes tedious as soon as the adaptation has more than a handful of files.

An automated approach

We looked for a more automated way and ended up with a small ABAP report that reads UI5 adaptation files directly from the layered repository and writes them to a local folder on your machine.

The important technical detail: files in the layered repository are persisted in database table /UIF/LREPDCONT. That table holds what you need to reconstruct a project on disk: the namespace (including the project identifier and folder structure), each file’s name and type, and the content in binary form.

The report: ZR_LREP_ADAPTATION_DOWNLOAD

We created report ZR_LREP_ADAPTATION_DOWNLOAD and kept it deliberately simple. It has a single main input: the custom app (variant) ID. You can type it or use value help. When you run the report and choose a target folder, the program downloads all related files and recreates the original folder structure on your local machine.

Limitation: dist folder only

You should be aware of one limitation: at present, only the contents of the dist (which is created as a result of the build step) folder are stored in the layered repository in a way this approach recovers. This is enough to recreate the project as these are same with the contents of the webapp folder. You still need to create a dummy UI5 adaptation project in your IDE and then move the downloaded  content to the webapp folder into that project so you do not miss files such as ui5.yaml & package.json.

Reference implementation

The full source for the report is available here:

https://github.com/onurakca/SAPUI5-Adaptation-Project-Downloader

The report is provided free of charge under the MIT License. Use it at your own risk: test in non-production systems first, verify behavior in your landscape, and ensure compliance with your organization’s rules for custom ABAP and repository access.

Conclusion

This post walked through how UI5 adaptations are tied to the layered repository rather than classic BSP download flows, how table /UIF/LREPDCONT stores the files you need, and how ZR_LREP_ADAPTATION_DOWNLOAD bulk-exports them while preserving project structure. You now know the gap between “deployment exists in SAP” and “IDE-ready project,” including the webapp-only limitation and the step of dropping files into a new adaptation shell. Even with that limitation, this can save a lot of time when you inherit an adaptation with no Git history and need to review, compare, or extend it quickly.

entry.title: New Practice System for Learning the Basics of SAP Fiori (UX100)
entry.content:
Last year, we took the practice system for the Learning the Basics of SAP Fiori (UX100) course offline to upgrade its capabilities to better reflect the latest additions to the course material. This was right before our latest innovation was kicked off internally: SAP Certifications reimagined.

Unfortunately, this shift of priorities led to some delay for this practice system. But now it is here: SAP Fiori – Foundation

  • You get full administrative access to an SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS04 like you would get in the classroom version of the UX100.
  • You get full administrative access to an SAP BTP subaccount with entitlements for SAP Business Application Studio and SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition.

All exercises can be done without any limitation:

  • Explore a fresh installed SAP S/4HANA with SAP Best Practices in place.
  • Use the fully configured SAP Fiori launchpad.
  • Set up SAP Fiori spaces, pages, and catalogs.
  • Dive into SAP Fiori development and system administration.

It is all here, just one booking away. Are you ready for some exercise(s)?

💡Hint:
UX100 is part of the learning journeys Developing SAPUI5 Applications and SAP Fiori – System Administration, which both lead to a certification. Check-out my blog post summarizing UX100.

entry.title: How I transformed my SAPUI5 development from code to visual
entry.content:
Introduction

Ever wondered if there's some way to draw SAPUI5 freestyle screens? Well, that was possible back in the days and I miss it. And because of that, I decided to make it possible again.

 

The matter in hand

I've been working with SAPUI5 since 2020, developing freestyle apps in the SAP WEB IDE (good old days). I started when Fiori Elements wasn't as mature as it is today, with just SEGW as a tool to create oData services.

Nowadays we have Visual Studio Code, and it's great! It's lightweight and has so many advantages compared to the early Fiori development tools. But I always felt that some things didn't evolve like I wanted to. I feel some features are missing, like the visual editor:

 

Ok, it's true it's better to create the view using XML code. But everyone should remember many Fiori developers are also ABAP developers. And honestly, ABAP developers are not usually very UI-focused. We loved Module Pool (kinda).

So, I decided to create something to help me out and thought "Why not everyone else". With this, I built a small open-source tool to explore this idea. It originated standalone as a web app, but now it's fully integrated with Vscode.

The idea is simple. Design a screen with drag-and-drop:

Generate an XML:

And also save as file inside the project. 

 

The reverse is also possible: open an existing view with the visual editor:

Also, one thing that I emphasized is the properties inspector. You can edit properties from a component visually:

There are some presets when you want a quick boilerplate:

 

And then we can test it with the preview application:

Sure, it's flawed yet. The extension is in active development, but there are a lot of features that can help the day-to-day development:

  • Open a visual UI5 screen designer directly inside VS Code.
  • Build SAPUI5/OpenUI5 screen wireframes using visual components.
  • Add components by clicking or dragging them onto the canvas.
  • Move, resize, duplicate, copy, paste, and delete components.
  • Select and manipulate multiple components at once.
  • Edit component properties through a visual inspector.
  • Generate XML View code automatically from the visual layout.
  • Copy the generated XML to the clipboard.
  • Save the XML as a new file inside the workspace.
  • Open an existing XML View from the VS Code context menu.
  • Preserve or infer the View controllerName when generating XML.
  • Generate responsive layouts, avoiding fixed pixel widths where possible.
  • Use UI5 layout patterns such as VBox, HBox, SimpleForm, FlexItemData, percentages, and rem spacing.
  • Automatically detect required namespaces such as f, core, uxap, and unified.
  • Preserve common bindings when importing and regenerating XML, including table bindings.
  • Suggest bindings based on local OData metadata when a manifest.json and metadata file are available.
  • Run the whole generation flow locally, without sending XML or wireframes to external services.
  • Create empty UI5 view with corresponding controller with just 1 click, automatically.

The extension is still in preview/development. Generated XML should be reviewed before being used in production, especially for complex or highly customized UI5 Views.

The extension is called UI5 Designer 

And also, the project is open source on GitHub 

 

Conclusion

So that's that. The project is still on development, but for me that's something I could use already. I hope it can help everyone designing, prototyping and maintaining SAPUI5 Views easier and visually.

I'd love to hear from everyone, and also improve it for the community.

entry.title: Fullstack CAP Application with HANA Cloud (Decoupled Architecture) [Part-1]
entry.content:
Developing the CAP Application Locally with SQLite

In this blog series, I will walk you through building an Incident Management application using SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) with Node.js, connected to SAP HANA Cloud, and deployed on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry environment.

The frontend is a Fiori Elements application deployed separately to the HTML5 Application Repository — making this a fully decoupled architecture.

Here is what we will cover across this series:

— Develop the CAP application locally using SQLite (this post)
— Connect the CAP app to SAP HANA Cloud
— Deploy the CAP backend to Cloud Foundry
— Build the Fiori Elements UI using SAP Fiori tools
— Configuring Destination in BTP
— Deploy the Fiori app to the HTML5 Application Repository

Before deploying anything to the cloud, it is always a good practice to build and test your CAP application locally. In this part, we will:

- Initialize the CAP project
- Define the domain model (entities)
- Create two OData services (Admin and Processor)
- Add sample data using CSV files
- Run and test the application locally using SQLite as the database

Let's get started!


PREREQUISITES

Make sure the following tools are installed and configured on your machine before proceeding:

- Node.js (v18 or higher recommended)
- @sap/cds-dk installed globally:

npm install -g @sap/cds-dk

- VS Code with the SAP CDS Language Support extension (recommended) / Business Application Studio (BAS)
- Basic familiarity with CAP and OData concepts is helpful


STEP 1 — CREATE THE CAP PROJECT

Open a terminal and run the following command to initialize a new CAP project with Node.js support:

cds init --add nodejs incident-management

This creates a new folder called "incident-management" with the standard CAP project structure.

Navigate into the project folder:

cd incident-management

Install the required Node.js dependencies:

npm install

 

After installation, your project structure will look like this:

incident-management/
├── db/ → Domain models (entities, types)
├── srv/ → Service definitions (OData services)
├── app/ → UI applications (used in later parts)
├── package.json
└── .cdsrc.json


STEP 2 — ADD THE DOMAIN MODEL

The domain model defines all the data entities for our application.
Inside the "db" folder, create a new file named: schema.cds

Paste the following code into schema.cds:

using {
  cuid,
  managed,
  sap.common.CodeList
} from '@sap/cds/common';

namespace sap.capire.incidents;

/**
* Incidents created by Customers.
*/
entity Incidents : cuid, managed {
  customer     : Association to Customers;
  title        : String @title: 'Title';
  urgency      : Association to Urgency default 'M';
  status       : Association to Status default 'N';
  conversation : Composition of many {
                   key ID        : UUID;
                       timestamp : type of managed : createdAt;
                       author    : type of managed : createdBy;
                       message   : String;
                 };
}

/**
* Customers entitled to create support Incidents.
*/
entity Customers : managed {
  key ID           : String;
      firstName    : String;
      lastName     : String;
      name         : String = trim(firstName || ' ' || lastName);
      email        : EMailAddress;
      phone        : PhoneNumber;
      incidents    : Association to many Incidents
                       on incidents.customer = $self;
      creditCardNo : String(16) @assert.format: '^[1-9]\d{15}$';
      addresses    : Composition of many Addresses
                       on addresses.customer = $self;
}

entity Addresses : cuid, managed {
  customer      : Association to Customers;
  city          : String;
  postCode      : String;
  streetAddress : String;
}

entity Status : CodeList {
  key code        : String enum {
        new = 'N';
        assigned = 'A';
        in_process = 'I';
        on_hold = 'H';
        resolved = 'R';
        closed = 'C';
      };
      criticality : Integer;
}

entity Urgency : CodeList {
  key code        : String enum {
        high = 'H';
        medium = 'M';
        low = 'L';
      };
      criticality : Integer;
}

type EMailAddress : String;
type PhoneNumber  : String;

The model uses the namespace: sap.capire.incidents

It imports three built-in CDS types from @sap/cds/common:

- cuid → automatically generates a UUID as the primary key.
- managed → automatically tracks createdAt, createdBy, modifiedAt, modifiedBy.
- CodeList → a base type for value-list entities like Status and Urgency.

Here is a description of each entity defined in the schema:

INCIDENTS
The core entity of the application. Each incident is raised by a Customer and has a title, an urgency level (default: Medium), and a status (default: New). It also contains an inline Composition of conversations — meaning each incident can have multiple conversation/comment entries stored together with it.

CUSTOMERS
Represents the customers who raise incidents. Stores their first name, last name, email, phone, and credit card number. The "name" field is a computed field that automatically combines firstName and lastName. Each customer can have multiple Addresses and multiple Incidents linked to them.

ADDRESSES
Stores the physical address of a customer, including city, postal code, and street address. It is a composition child of Customers.

STATUS
A code list entity representing the lifecycle stages of an incident:
N → New
A → Assigned
I → In Process
H → On Hold
R → Resolved
C → Closed
Each status has a criticality value used for UI color coding in Fiori.

URGENCY
A code list entity representing the priority level of an incident:
H → High
M → Medium
L → Low
Each urgency level also carries a criticality value for UI color coding.


STEP 3 — CREATE THE SERVICES

CAP uses service definitions to expose your entities as OData endpoints.
We will create two separate service files inside the "srv" folder.

a) incidents_admin.cds

This service is used by administrators to manage customers and incidents. It exposes the Customers entity (with OData Draft enabled for edit flows) and the Incidents entity.

The service is published at the path: /odata/v4/incidents-admin

using {sap.capire.incidents as my} from '../db/schema';


/**
 * Service used by administrators to manage customers and incidents.
 */

 : 'incidents-admin'
service AdminService {
    .draft.enabled
    entity Customers as projection on my.Customers;
    entity Incidents as projection on my.Incidents;
}


b) incidents_processor.cds

This service is used by support personnel (incident processors). It exposes the Incidents entity (with OData Draft enabled) as the main working entity. The Customers entity is auto-exposed as a read-only entity — meaning processors can view customer data when working on an incident, but cannot modify it.

The service is published at the path: /odata/v4/incidents-processor

using {sap.capire.incidents as my} from '../db/schema';

/**
 * Service used by support personell, i.e. the incidents' 'processors'.
 */


@path: 'incidents-processor'
service ProcessorService  {
    .draft.enabled
    entity Incidents as projection on my.Incidents;

    // 
    // entity Customers as projection on my.Customers;

    annotate my.Customers with .autoexpose; // Exposes Customers entity as read only entity
}


> WHY TWO SERVICES?
Separating admin and processor services is a best practice in CAP. It lets you assign different authorization roles to each service and expose only what each user role actually needs. This follows the  principle of least privilege and makes it easier to apply security restrictions later (covered in a future part of this series).


STEP 4 — GENERATE CSV TEMPLATES AND ADD INITIAL DATA

CAP provides a built-in command to auto-generate CSV template files based on your domain model.

Run:

cds add data

This creates empty CSV files inside a newly created "db/data" folder.

The file names follow the pattern: <namespace>-<EntityName>.csv
e.g., sap.capire.incidents-Customers.csv

CAP automatically picks up these files and loads them as seed/initial data every time the application starts in development mode.

Now add the following sample data to each file:

a) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Addresses.csv
Contains address records for each customer (city, postal code, street).

ID,customer_ID,city,postCode,streetAddress
17e00347-dc7e-4ca9-9c5d-06ccef69f064,1004155,Rome,00164,Piazza Adriana
d8e797d9-6507-4aaa-b43f-5d2301df5135,1004161,Munich,80809,Olympia Park
ff13d2fa-e00f-4ee5-951c-3303f490777b,1004100,Walldorf,69190,Dietmar-Hopp-Allee


b) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Customers.csv
Contains three sample customers with ID, name, email, and phone.

ID,firstName,lastName,email,phone
1004155,Daniel,Watts,daniel.watts@demo.com,+39-555-123
1004161,Stormy,Weathers,stormy.weathers@demo.com,+49-020-022
1004100,Sunny,Sunshine,sunny.sunshine@demo.com,+49-555-789


c) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Incidents.conversation.csv
Contains conversation/comment entries linked to incidents via the
"up__ID" foreign key (CAP convention for composition parent).

ID,up__ID,timestamp,author,message
2b23bb4b-4ac7-4a24-ac02-aa10cabd842c,3b23bb4b-4ac7-4a24-ac02-aa10cabd842c,1995-12-17T03:24:00Z,Harry John,Can you please check if battery connections are fine?
2b23bb4b-4ac7-4a24-ac02-aa10cabd843c,3a4ede72-244a-4f5f-8efa-b17e032d01ee,1995-12-18T04:24:00Z,Emily Elizabeth,Can you please check if there are any loose connections?
9583f982-d7df-4aad-ab26-301d4a157cd7,3583f982-d7df-4aad-ab26-301d4a157cd7,2022-09-04T12:00:00Z,Sunny Sunshine,Please check why the solar panel is broken.
9583f982-d7df-4aad-ab26-301d4a158cd7,3ccf474c-3881-44b7-99fb-59a2a4668418,2022-09-04T13:00:00Z,Bradley Flowers,What exactly is wrong?


d) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Incidents.csv
Contains four sample incidents with title, urgency code, status code, and a reference to the customer who raised them.

ID,customer_ID,title,urgency_code,status_code
3b23bb4b-4ac7-4a24-ac02-aa10cabd842c,1004155,Inverter not functional,H,C
3a4ede72-244a-4f5f-8efa-b17e032d01ee,1004161,No current on a sunny day,H,N
3ccf474c-3881-44b7-99fb-59a2a4668418,1004161,Strange noise when switching off Inverter,M,N
3583f982-d7df-4aad-ab26-301d4a157cd7,1004100,Solar panel broken,H,I


e) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Status.csv
Contains the status code list: N, A, I, H, R, C with descriptions and criticality values.

code,descr,criticality
N,New,0
A,Assigned,0
I,In Process,2
H,On Hold,0
R,Resolved,3
C,Closed,1


f) db/data/sap.capire.incidents-Urgency.csv
Contains the urgency code list: H, M, L with descriptions and criticality values.

code,descr,criticality
H,High,1
M,Medium,2
L,Low,3


STEP 5 — RUN THE PROJECT WITH SQLITE

First, deploy the schema to a local SQLite database file:

cds deploy --to sqlite

Then start the development server with live-reload:

cds watch

Once the server starts, open your browser and go to: http://localhost:4004 in your browser.

You should see the CDS welcome page listing all available OData service endpoints:

 

The two services and their exposed entities will be listed:

/odata/v4/incidents-admin
→ Customers (with Fiori preview)
→ Incidents (with Fiori preview)

/odata/v4/incidents-processor
→ Incidents (with Fiori preview)
→ Customers (with Fiori preview — read-only)

You can click the "Fiori preview" link next to any entity to open an auto-generated Fiori Elements UI — useful for quick testing without building a full UI yet.

You can also directly access the raw OData feed in the browser.

For example: http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/incidents-processor/Incidents

This will return the incident records from your CSV seed data in JSON format, confirming that the data was loaded correctly.

 


SUMMARY

In Part 1, we successfully:

✔ Created a CAP Node.js project using cds init
✔ Defined the domain model with Incidents, Customers, Addresses, Conversations, Status, and Urgency entities
✔ Created two OData services — AdminService and ProcessorService —with appropriate entity exposure for each user role
✔ Added initial seed data using CSV files
✔ Ran and validated the application locally using SQLite

The application is fully functional locally. In Part 2 , we will replace SQLite with SAP HANA Cloud and prepare the project for cloud deployment.

entry.title: SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA - What’s New in Moving from Standalone to Embedded Deployment [V3.0]
entry.content:
SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA – What’s New in the 2026 Update of Moving from Standalone to Embedded Deployment

As you may already know, SAP continues to recommend deploying SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA using an embedded SAP Fiori Front-End Server.

Over the years, many customers who initially started with a standalone FES have been transitioning to embedded deployment to reduce complexity, optimise operations, and align with SAP’s long term architecture.
Looking ahead, this direction becomes even more explicit. As outlined in the SAP Fiori Deployment Options and System Landscape Recommendations  (as of Guide’s Version 10.3, February 2026) under the section Outlook: Fiori Deployment, the guide explicitly states:

SAP Fiori Deployment Options and System Landscape Recommendations, Section 'Outlook: Fiori Deployment''

“Please be aware that as of SAP S/4HANA 2027 the embedded deployment option will be the only available deployment option”. This effectively makes embedded deployment not just the recommended approach, but the standard going forward for SAP Fiori in SAP S/4HANA landscapes.

In this context, we’re excited to share the updated “Moving from Standalone to Embedded Deployment in SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA”  guide, based on SAP S/4HANA 2025, which becomes even more relevant for customers planning or progressing through this transition.

The guide was first published in October 2019 (version 1.0). It has since evolved through multiple updates, including version 2.1 in December 2024, which focused on SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA 2023. The most recent version, 3.0 released in March 2026, is based on SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA 2025.

Moving from Standalone to Embedded Deployment in SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA (V3.0)

 This latest update (Version 3.0, March 2026) has been driven with strong input from SAP product experts, including Peter Persiel (peterpersiel) , whose contributions have helped shape the guide based on real-world customer experiences and evolving SAP Fiori architecture.

So what actually changed?

This blog highlights the latest updates and key enhancements introduced in the 2026 version of the guide (Version 3.0 March 2026), helping you quickly understand what’s new, what’s improved, and what to consider for your projects going forward.

Why this update matters?

Version 3.0 of this guide is an important refresh. It reflects:

  • Alignment with SAP S/4HANA 2025 and SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA 2025, and important announcements planned in the future releases of SAP S/4HANA.
  • The continued evolution of SAP Fiori architecture and activation tooling, especially in embedded deployments
  • Lessons learned from more recent customer implementations, translating real project experience into more practical guidance
  • Increased focus on clean core, maintainability, and lifecycle management

Why do you need this guide?

This guide is essential for SAP S/4HANA customers currently running or considering a hub/standalone SAP Fiori Front-End Server deployment, and who are planning or preparing for a transition from a hub/standalone SAP Fiori Front-End Server deployment to an embedded deployment.

It provides clear, structured guidance to support a smooth transition, covering both technical and practical aspects of the move. Whether you are upgrading your SAP S/4HANA system or proactively aligning with SAP’s long term architectural direction, this guide helps you plan and execute the transition with confidence.

From planning, installation, and configuration to post migration validation and optimisation, it brings together everything you need to successfully move to an embedded SAP Fiori deployment.

It helps you:

  • Understand the benefits of moving to embedded FES deployment
  • Know the right time to make the shift
  • Navigate technical prerequisites, sizing, and system considerations
  • Simplify the migration process with actionable steps, tools, and examples
  • Ensure a seamless experience for users by resolving common issues
  • Minimise disruptions by retaining user personalisation and role assignments
  • Gain tips on how to enhance usability and performance

By following this guide, you can align with SAP best practices and unlock the full potential of SAP Fiori embedded deployment.

What's in it / What's new?

  • Simplified architecture in SAP S/4HANA 2025
    Some Front-end components are now part of the core platform, reducing system complexity
  • More practical, end-to-end guidance
    Covers not just migration steps, but also planning, validation, and long term operations
  • Better control of custom content migration
    Helps define what custom content to move and adds validation after migration instead of blindly transporting everything
  • Improved transport strategy guidance
    Real world options for handling transports depending on your landscape and ongoing development activities
  • Extended launchpad configuration coverage
    Includes transport of launchpad parameters, plug ins, and configuration settings beyond just apps and catalogs
  • Built-in validation step
    Dedicated step to validate custom content in the embedded system after migration
  • Enhanced personalisation migration
    Clear guidance using tools like /UIF/MIGRATE_FES_PERSO to retain user settings, homepages, and UI changes
  • Explicit system alias and RFC adjustments
    Clear steps to adjust system aliases and RFC destinations post migration
  • Smarter SAP Notes handling
    Explicit step to identify and implement missing SAP Notes after migration, avoiding common post go live issues
  • Stronger focus on business roles
    Additional step to review and optimise business roles after migration in line with modern Fiori design

Becoming a SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA guru

You’ll find much more on our  SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA topic page

Other helpful links:

  • SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA – Foundational SAP Notes for SAP S/4HANA 2025
  • See all questions and answers about SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA 
  • Follow SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA for more blogs and updates 
  • Ask a Question about SAP Fiori for SAP S/4HANA

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA RIG and Customer Care team.

entry.title: Accelerating SAP Fiori & CAP Development With SAP MCP Server + GitHub Copilot in VS Code
entry.content:
☕The 5-Hour CAP App That Now Takes 30 Minutes

Last month, I sat down with one of our experienced SAP CAP developers and asked her to build a small Fiori Elements app on top of a CAP Java service — start to first working screen. She'd done dozens of these. It took her just under five hours.

A week later, I asked a colleague — a senior ABAP developer who'd never written a line of CDS or Spring Boot — to build the same thing using GitHub Copilot Chat plus the new SAP MCP Server in VS Code.

He shipped a working app — CAP service with one entity, Fiori Elements List Report + Object Page, draft handling, basic security — in 38 minutes

That gap isn't a fluke. It's the new reality for SAP development — and in this post I want to walk you through what changed, how the pieces fit together, and how to set this up yourself.

🎯Why This Matters Right Now

If you build on SAP, you've felt the friction:

  • 🐌Annotations are unforgiving. A single misplaced UI.LineItem token can swallow an afternoon.
  • 📚Documentation is fragmented across cap.cloud.sap, help.sap.com, blogs, and Discovery Center.
  • ☕CAP Java handlers require Spring Boot fluency that ABAP developers don't have on day one.
  • 🔌BTP wiring — mta.yaml, xs-security.json, destinations, role collections — is unforgiving and project-after-project repetitive.

That's exactly what changed in the last few months.

🧩 The Three Pieces That Snap Together

The breakthrough isn't any one tool — it's three components working as a system.

1️⃣Visual Studio Code

The neutral home base. Every SAP toolchain — CAP, Fiori Tools, Cloud MTA Build Tool, the BTP CLI — already lives comfortably here.

2️⃣GitHub Copilot Chat (Enterprise)

Not "Copilot suggests an autocomplete." This is the conversational, context-aware Copilot that can read your workspace, plan multi-step changes, and call external tools.

3️⃣SAP's MCP Servers (Three of Them)

This is the new piece. MCP — Model Context Protocol — is an open standard that lets AI assistants invoke external tools through a standard interface.

 

When Copilot Chat in Agent mode calls these servers, you get something neither tool delivers alone: an AI that understands CAP- and Fiori-specific patterns rather than producing generic "make me a Java app" output.

🔑 The mental model: GitHub Copilot is the brain. SAP MCP servers give it SAP-shaped hands.

📜 But Wait — Is This Actually Allowed Under the SAP API Policy?

It's the first question every architect and CoE lead asks the moment MCP enters the conversation — and rightly so. The SAP API Policy (published in the SAP Help Portal, latest FAQ v1.1, May 2026) sets clear guardrails for AI- and agent-driven access to SAP APIs.

So let's address it directly: yes, the three MCP servers used here are explicitly endorsed by SAP.

🟢 The Direct Quote

From Question 37 of the SAP API Policy FAQ:

"SAP-managed and SAP-published MCP servers (for example, the CAP MCP Server, UI5 MCP Server, and Fiori Elements MCP Server for developer tooling) are themselves endorsed. MCP Gateway on SAP Integration Suite … is also an endorsed path for managed MCP access to SAP endpoints subject to the compliance with the API Policy…"

🛠 Setting Up the Stack (≈ 15 minutes)

Step 1 — Prerequisites Check

Before we configure MCP, verify:

  1. node -v :: Should be v20.x or higher
  2. npm -v :: Should be 10.x or higher
  3. npx --version :: Should work

Step 2 — Install VS Code Extensions

  • SAP CDS Language Support
  • SAP Fiori Tools – Extension Pack
  • GitHub Copilot + GitHub Copilot Chat
  • Extension Pack for Java 

Step 3 — Create Your Workspace and MCP Configuration

In your workspace, create .vscode/mcp.json:

Paste this exact content:

​

Step 4 — Start the MCP Servers

​Open Command Palette: Ctrl + Shift + P

​Type: MCP: List Servers → press Enter

​You'll see all servers listed here on right hand side.

​For each server, click → Start Server

​​Step 5 — Enable Agent Mode in Copilot

​Open Copilot Chat (Ctrl + Alt + I)

​Bottom of chat input → mode dropdown → switch to Agent

​Click 🛠tools icon →  cap, fiori, ui5 tools appear

That's it. You're ready.

Your First "Wow" Prompt

Open Copilot Chat in Agent mode and try this:

"Use the cap MCP server to initialize a new CAP Java project named scheduling-agreements with the SQLite profile, namespace com.example.scheduling, and configure mock users admin/admin and viewer/viewer in application.yaml."

In about 20 seconds you'll have a fully wired project: db/, srv/, app/, a Maven pom.xml, an application.yaml with mock users, and a .gitignore that won't betray your local SQLite database into source control.

Now follow up:

"Create entity SchedulingAgreement with fields ID (UUID, key), vendor (String 50, mandatory), plant (String 4), validFrom (Date), validTo (Date), totalValue (Decimal 15,2) with Currency. Include the managed aspect."

You'll get production-quality CDS — properly typed, annotated with @title/@description, currency association from @sap/cds/common, all the small craftsmanship details an experienced CAP dev would apply.

Then:

"Define a service exposing this entity, and use the fiori MCP server to scaffold a Fiori Elements List Report + Object Page module with i18n in en, de, es, pt-BR."

About 90 seconds later, you can run mvn spring-boot:run, open the Fiori preview, and see a working app.

entry.title: SAP User Experience in SAP Cloud ERP 2602
entry.content:
The SAP Cloud ERP 2602 release (fka SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition) has been shipped on January 14, 2026.

With every release update, our goal is to make your interaction with the system more intuitive, efficient, and intelligent. The 2602 release is a significant step forward, focusing on consistency, AI-powered assistance, enhanced personalization, and on-the-go mobility. Let's dive into the highlights in the area of SAP User Experience!

The new shell bar is here to stay

Remember the redesigned shell bar we introduced as an opt-in feature back in 2508? Based on positive feedback and our drive for a unified experience, it is now activated by default in the 2602 release.

The new shell bar provides:

  • An updated user menu: With a larger user image and more space between items, finding what you need is easier than ever. We've also moved "Settings" to the top and separated "Sign Out" for quicker access.

     

  • Redesigned notifications: A simplified, more pleasing design with easy sorting (by date or importance) and simple grouping (Today, Yesterday, etc.) helps you stay on top of your tasks without the clutter.

Important note for a smooth transition: As this is now the default, we strongly recommend you check any test automation or RPA scripts you have, as they may need to be adapted to the new user menu structure. For this release only, you can temporarily opt-out via the "Activate New Features" switch (feature `/UI2/SHELL_BAR_TOGGLE`), but please plan to adapt your systems, as the opt-out option will not be available from the 2608 release onwards.

Direct app launch in the SAP Fiori launchpad

We've heard you! Many of our experienced users coming from the SAP GUI world missed the ability to quickly launch an application using its transaction code. I'm delighted to announce that you can now launch apps directly from the SAP Fiori launchpad search bar using the app ID or transaction code.


For power users, this restores a familiar and efficient navigation pattern, lowering the barrier for those transitioning to SAP Fiori, as they can combine new UX principles with known shortcuts.

Enhancements to My Home

My Home continues to evolve as your central, personalized starting point. In 2602, we're giving you more control and enhancing communication:

  • Required reading for news: Administrators can now mark important news articles, such as new policies or critical process changes, as "required reading." Users must acknowledge these articles, which helps ensure compliance and consistent knowledge across the organization.

  • Greater layout control: You can now choose to hide the entire "News" or "Pages" sections on your My Home screen. This allows you to reduce clutter and tailor your homepage to display only the information and tools that are most relevant to your role, boosting your personal productivity.



An intelligent assistant for your daily tasks

Artificial intelligence is at the heart of the intelligent enterprise, and we're embedding it directly into your user experience.

The 2602 release distinguishes between base and premium AI features:

  • Base features (free of charge): After signing the acceptance of SAP AI terms for base AI-assisted features in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, you gain access to features like AI-assisted enterprise search, easy filter, and smart summarization.
  • Premium features (requires license): With the appropriate licenses (like SAP AI units and package Joule Premium for financal management), you can unlock advanced capabilities. In 2602, we are excited to announce the general availability (GA) of AI-assisted smart personalization of applications in My Home and AI-assisted error explanation.

Furthermore, we are continuing to beta test AI-assisted task automation, which allows users to create "smart helpers" to automate repetitive activities. This powerful tool keeps the user in the loop while dramatically improving efficiency. If you're interested in shaping the future of AI in our products, I encourage you to sign up for our beta testing program here: https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#campaign/4086

Fast with SAP Fiori

We’re thrilled to announce the launch of our brand-new video series, "Fast with SAP Fiori"! The first three episodes are now live and ready to accelerate your SAP Fiori journey.

What’s coming:

  • Complete 10-episode series covering essential SAP Fiori concepts
  • Soon available on YouTube for easy access
  • Multi-language support including English, German, French, Spanish, Portuguese, and Italian

Ready to dive in? Check out the first three episodes now, and stay tuned for the complete series launch!

  • Video 1 - https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/ce7RMfU?rc=10&doi=SAP1286910 
  • Video 2 - https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/Ef5HP5X?rc=10&doi=SAP1286912 
  • Video 3 - https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/558Yrrv?rc=10&doi=SAP1286922 

Productivity on the go: SAP Fiori for mobile optimization

In today's flexible work environment, being productive on your smartphone is essential. That's why we've introduced SAP Fiori elements mobile phone optimization. This delivers a mobile-first experience by providing a compact, modern, and high-performance framework for key mobile scenarios. We've simplified navigation and reduced complexity by focusing on what's essential for users on the move, allowing you to get business done anywhere, anytime. Feel free to sign up for our beta testing program here: https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#campaign/4090

A single front door: Introducing SAP Start (SAP-managed)

Imagine a single, unified landing page for all your SAP Cloud ERP products. That is the vision for SAP Start, and with the 2602 release, we are introducing a new SAP-managed version.

This means you can activate it directly from SAP for Me with no additional implementation effort. In its initial release, SAP Start provides seamless, single sign-on access to SAP Cloud ERP, SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Concur, and SAP Sales Cloud Version 2. This is just the beginning, and we are eager for your feedback through our Early Adopter Care program to shape its future.

 

Your voice matters: Get involved!

Many of the features in this release, including the highly-requested direct app launch, originated as suggestions on the SAP Customer Influence Portal (CIP.) This is proof that your feedback directly shapes the future of our products.

I encourage you to explore these new features, see how they fit your business needs, and continue to share your insights with us through the SAP Community and the Customer Influence portal.

The 2602 release truly elevates the SAP User Experience, and we can't wait to see how you leverage these innovations to run your business better.

📽️ Watch the replays of our SAP Cloud ERP 2602 Early Release Series! 

From January 12-16, we hosted a series of 22 expert-led live sessions to highlight the exciting innovations shipped with the SAP Cloud ERP 2602 release. Missed the live sessions? We've got you covered! Take advantage of the entire series or pick and choose the sessions most relevant to you. You will find the recording and presentation for SAP User Experience as well as 21 other topics of interest. Don't miss this chance and watch them on demand–anywhere, anytime. 

🔎 Find all our assets 

We at Cloud ERP and SCM Product Success offer a service as versatile as our product itself. Check out the numerous offerings our team has created for you below:

entry.title: Upgrading your SAPUI5 Rich Text Editor: Beyond TinyMCE version 4
entry.content:
Rich Text Editor in SAPUI5

The Rich Text Editor is an SAPUI5 control for data input and editing. It is used to enter rich text with different styles, colors, and formatting. The Rich Text Editor allows users to format text and add different types of elements, such as images and hyperlinks.

Screenshot of the SAPUI5 Rich Text Editor control

See details on

  • sap.ui.richtexteditor.RichTextEditor: Design, documentation, API reference and samples
  • RichTextEditor building block: Documentation and documentation in SAP Fiori Development Portal

Dependency on TinyMCE

For its implementation, this control uses the third-party component TinyMCE (see https://www.tiny.cloud/). When Tiny deprecates a version of TinyMCE, this version might still be in use in some versions of SAPUI5. Should issues arise, the SAPUI5 team tries to fix them by working around gaps. However, sometimes this is not possible, and updating TinyMCE is necessary, even in maintenance versions of SAPUI5. This is especially important for security, an area where SAPUI5 must address upcoming risks for all customers.

This dependency adds restrictions to the usage and support of the Rich Text Editor control, see the documentation. If your app uses a deprecated version of TinyMCE, the console shows a respective error message stating that this version of TinyMCE is deprecated and will be removed in future.

What happens in July 2026?

 

SAPUI5 will stop loading the deprecated TinyMCE version 4 by default.
Instead, it will load the latest available version.

This will happen in the following SAPUI5 versions:

  • SAPUI5 1.71   - TinyMCE deprecated as of 1.71.62
  • SAPUI5 1.84   - TinyMCE deprecated as of 1.84.42
  • SAPUI5 1.96   - TinyMCE deprecated as of 1.96.28
  • SAPUI5 1.108 - TinyMCE deprecated as of 1.108.0 

What does this mean for you?

As always, you benefit from improved features and qualities of the SAPUI5 Rich Text Editor. If, and only if, your app relies on TinyMCE version 4, the app will break until you use the EditorType property to explicitly stay with version 4. SAP does not recommend using deprecated software, as explained above.

What is planned for 2026?

The SAPUI5 team further plans to permanently remove TinyMCE version 4 from the SAPUI5 code later in 2026. This means there will be no way to load it anymore, no will workaround available. SAP strongly recommends removing any dependency to TinyMCE version 4.

Call to Action

Avoid any dependency on a specific (deprecated) version of TinyMCE to always benefit from the latest features and qualities in your SAPUI5 apps.

entry.title: Issue in Standard Fiori App F1780 – “CO Area is Not Set” (Cost Accountant – Production)
entry.content:
Hi Folks,

I am writing this blog after analyzing the issue in detail and discussing it with the SAP Product Team. I hope this helps others resolve the issue quickly and efficiently.

 

Issue Description:

We activated the standard Fiori application F1780 – Cost Accountant – Production in our S/4HANA system. However, after configuring the app in the Fiori Launchpad (FLP), the following error appeared directly on the application tile:

"CO Area is not set"

 

Additionally, when clicking the tile and attempting to launch the application, the system displayed a warning message and did not allow further navigation.

 

Analyzing:

During troubleshooting, we identified that the issue was related to the Controlling Area assignment in the Fiori Launchpad user profile.

SAP Note referenced:

2403512 – Controlling area is not set for the user in Fiori Launchpad

 

Based on the note, we maintained the Controlling Area in the FLP user settings/profile. However, even after maintaining the configuration, the same error and warning continued to appear.

 

Solution:

After further discussion with the SAP Product Team, they provided the correct resolution and created the following KBA:

3753053 – F1780: “Controlling Area is not set” error in Fiori Launchpad

As per the KBA, the issue can be resolved by maintaining the required parameter in transaction SU01.

Steps to Fix

Navigate to:

SU01 → User Profile → Parameters

Maintain the following entry:

  • SET/GET Parameter ID: CAC

  • Parameter Value: 8400 (Use your relevant Controlling Area value)

 

After saving the changes:

  1. Log out from the Fiori Launchpad
  2. Log in again

The error message on the tile will disappear, and the application will open successfully with the expected data.

 

 

After Clicking on the TIle, it will show the data

 

Conclusion:

The issue is not resolved only by maintaining the Controlling Area in FLP personalization settings. The crucial step is maintaining the CAC parameter in SU01 for the respective user.

I hope this blog saves time for others facing the same issue.

Please feel free to share your suggestions or additional findings.

Regards,
Rohit Gera

entry.title: Sapphire 2026. The future of UX is here. Are you ready for an AI -powered world?
entry.content:
Sapphire 2026 accelerated SAP’s pivot to AI. Most of the keynote demos were heavy on AI, including introducing new engagement layer experiences to complement the existing application experiences. The magic of the engagement layer is that it allows you to interact with your ERP system in a conversational way, starting with your intent and the system will generate an interface based on the context of your request. While this might represent a more natural way to work, it also represents a major change to how people have been working with SAP applications for the past few decades.

The new engagement layer lets you interact with SAP in a conversational way.

I had the opportunity to see many deep-dive sessions that explained the new UX and how we expect people to work with it. More importantly, I spoke with many customers and partners about their reaction to the way we are moving. Generally, people are excited about the direction we are going and having more automation to free up time for deeper thought and analysis, but they are concerned about trusting AI to do things that might have significant business impact. While Joule Work will certainly be a game changer for many, the big open question is how to get there. As with all IT transformations, it will involve a combination of changes across people, processes, and technology.

Embedded AI meets customers where they are

The first session that I presented was on “Creating better user experiences with AI-powered SAP Fiori apps ASUG.” I started out by talking about overcoming usability challenges with AI and then did a live demo of around 10 cool productivity features. What’s unique about the embedded AI capabilities in SAP Cloud ERP is that it is built into the existing interfaces that people know, so it allows them to go through their work more quickly by using AI to search, summarize, and enter data.

There are many AI-powered features that make people more productive when using SAP Cloud ERP.

The slides from the session (PUX1228) include links where you can see short recorded demos of these features. These capabilities are available for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. Some of them are coming to private cloud in upcoming releases.

Some questions that customers asked that might be of general interest:

  • Does the engagement layer replace SAP Fiori? No – the application experience and engagement layer experience are complementary. They will coexist for the foreseeable future. (Thanks for the question, Tony)
  • Does the engagement layer use SAP Fiori elements? The answer here is more nuanced. We use the OData metadata that is used by Fiori elements to get the SAP S/4HANA data to render high quality cards in the engagement layer.
    So, the engagement layer is independent of the Fiori apps themselves. The engagement layer does not call the Fiori apps for the data, but it uses the same underlying data source, which are the OData services and annotations. (Great question, Kelly)

Lots of people were interested in having a better user experience with AI assistance.

Fear, optimism, and the AI Skills gap

My other presentation was a “community conversation” in the ASUG Hub. My colleague, Nicole Smythe and I facilitated the session along with Liz Tuckwell and Nadalee Williams from ASUG. The goal was to collect ideas on AI adoption from the attendees and equip them with information so they could create a 90-day plan to turn their thoughts into action and move forward with AI in your organization. After providing an overview of Joule Work, we broke into teams and people discussed barriers to implementing AI in their organizations and how they overcame them.

Top problems that the audience identified included:

  • Data. How to get the data clean so any AI agents can work properly and consistently. Most people know they need to fix their business processes and clean their data before they can benefit from AI.
  • Governance. People see the need for IT to manage this centrally. Currently, many innovative teams want to get started with AI and are doing things on their own without corporate oversight. We saw this situation a decade ago when some business managers purchased cloud services for sales or HR or other domains if they did not receive sufficient support from their IT teams.
  • Cost. How to meter AI consumption and allocate charges to different users.
  • AI Expertise. Upskilling teams on AI and its capabilities. Currently, this is a side job for most people, so they are not putting enough focus on this. Once company had a rotation program where top developers could allocate 50% of their time to learning AI topics and then bring those skills back to their teams.
  • Talent management. With fewer entry-level workers or people doing repetitive jobs, it is harder to identify the best employees, so you can focus on retention and building a leadership pipeline.

Participants discussed how to overcome barriers to AI adoption.

Other interesting sessions on AI, UX, and AI+UX

Most of the other sessions I attended were on AI, UX, or their intersection. Hearing the questions people asked and what they talked about afterwards will be helpful in refining our agenda for our next SAP Connect Day for UX, which is in Palo Alto on June 11. We’ll go deep on our UX strategy and share some new functionality, both released and unreleased. There will be two hands-on exercises, one on building applications and the other building Smart Helpers (currently in beta).

  • Katja Zoch and Sebastian Steinhauer had a session on how to extend SAP S/4HANA Cloud while keeping a clean core. They focused on what is clean core, why its important, and how to extend your core ERP system using clean core principles.  
  • Chris Hoffmann had several sessions on the show floor. They all focused on different dimensions of the AI-powered user experience in the SAP Business Suite. One was a broad survey of functionality, another focused on AI-assisted task automation, and the other talked about roadmap items. All showed how AI adoption is on the path towards human‑supervised autonomous ERP.
  • Tom Reiss presented on SAP Fiori in action, highlighting how modern UX helps users get their work done more easily and efficiently. He also had a roadmap session with Christina Salwizek featuring web and mobile innovations that are on the horizon.
  • Sophia Levens had some great sessions on the new Joule user experience, focusing on both how it works and the implications for users. She also had a strategy-focused talk with SAP’s chief designer officer, Arin Bhowmick, where they explored where user engagement and enterprise UX are headed.
  • Bert Schultz and Esther Vrieze spoke about different ways to migrate to SAP S/4HANA and the relative benefits among the different approaches.
  • Maura Hameroff and Rudi Hois talked about SAP Cloud ERP Private and the many innovations that customers can expect to see in upcoming releases.

We held our SAP Fiori Development Roundtable live from the show

To add a little extra energy to our already lively discussions, I joined my co-host Sebastian Werner live from the show floor. We spent lots of time talking about Joule Studio and how it fits with our existing development tools. If you want to join the next monthly call, please register.

AI vibe from across the conference

While the sessions are a great way to learn about hot topics, the best way to hear about how customers are using our solutions and what they are planning is in the hallways, food areas, UXpresso Café (successor to the Fiori Café), happy hours, and demo pods. The questions after sessions are also revealing. This year, the customers I spoke to were concerned about:

  • How the new engagement layer (Joule Work) fits with SAP Fiori
  • How to get started with AI, especially in private cloud and on-prem environments
  • If their existing Fiori apps will continue to work (yes!) and if they should continue to build new Fiori apps (yes!)
  • All the topics listed above from the ASUG community conversation (data, governance, cost, AI skills, and talent pipeline)

Next steps

Perhaps you are still digesting all the announcements from Sapphire and figuring out their impact on your business. To help you prepare for the future, consider discussing the following questions with your team.

  1. What business problems are most pressing and can benefit from automation? How will we meter the costs, set appropriate limits, and measure the return on investment?
  2. Is our data clean, consistent across different parts of the business, ready to train the AI system, and be part of automated workflows? How much autonomy are we willing to give agents and what is the best way to supervise / manage / audit their activities?
  3. How will we integrate AI into our SAP landscape? Are we using SAP’s embedded capabilities, Joule, the new engagement layer, or external AI-powered apps running on SAP data (subject to the API policy)?
  4. How will we address change management with our business users?
  5. How will we manage AI governance, risk, and security? Using SAP’s AI simplifies the answer to this question as we cover much of this, but you still need robust AI policies in your organization

If we did not meet at Sapphire and you are interested in joining the conversation about SAP Fiori application development and how AI makes it better, faster, and cheaper, please join our monthly SAP Fiori development roundtable group or sign up for our bi-monthly newsletter.

If you are in the Silicon Valley area or can fly there on June 11, please join us for SAP Connect Day for UX. We'll go deeper into all the UX and related AI topics listed in this post.
https://events.sap.com/us-ux-innovation-day-siliconvalley-2026/en_us/home.html 

For the UX Foundation team, Peter Spielvogel.

entry.title: SAP UX Update: The New Joule Work Engagement Layer Experiences
entry.content:
What is Joule Work, and what does it mean for you? What do the new Joule Work engagement layer experiences mean for existing application experiences? How can you try it out? Read on to get an overview of Joule Work and answers to these questions.

What is Joule Work?

Joule Work provides an AI-based user experience that transforms how work happens. With Joule work, people express what they want to accomplish, and Joule surfaces the right insights, automates routine work, and coordinates AI agents across systems to achieve it – all in one dynamic workspace. It acts as an engagement layer that spans across SAP products and beyond, and coexists with current experiences provided by pre-defined application UIs.

It represents a new way of interacting with business systems, since it provides:

  • One single place where a user can get tasks done, rather than the traditional app-centric interaction model, where users need to navigate to different applications or even to different products.
  • An intent-driven, situational experience, where users state their intents in natural language and the system builds on the fly accordingly, taking the current business and user context (the current situation) into account: fetching data, triggering AI agents, and visualizing the outcome appropriately.

Figure 1: Joule Work experiences: Conversations, Spaces, and Develop. Alt Text: The image shows 3 screenshots, one each for the three experiences. The top of the image has a heading  “Joule Work”, above a row with five attributes: Dynamic, Proactive, Agentic, Intent-based, Omnipresent.

Joule Work provides these experiences:

  • Conversations: this experience allows people to express intent and explore what they want to accomplish through dialogue with Joule. It is an AI-powered chat supporting role-based intents, multiple conversation threads, with type-ahead, auto-suggestions and multi-facetted types of responses (text, tables, charts etc.). Joule brings together guidance and outcomes from across SAP and third-party systems. Conversations can also act as an entry point to Spaces for more complex workflows.
  • Spaces: provides flexible AI-generated spaces that dynamically adapt content based on user intent and context, for dealing with simple as well as complex tasks and issues. Each space is composed in real time to bring the right data, tools, and context together for a specific goal, allowing people to work in an environment that adapts to their present goal, instead of navigating applications and their pre-defined UIs.​
  • Develop: this experience is provided by Joule Studio, enabling developers to vibe-code, test and deploy AI agents, applications, and automations across SAP and third-party systems. Joule Work provides a convenient entry point to Joule Studio for developers and business users alike, giving them an AI-powered user experience for pro-code and low-code tools.

To get a better idea of these experiences, have a look at Joule Work in action:

Click on the image to start the video.

Joule Work mobile app

The Joule Work mobile app provides Joule Work Conversations and Spaces for mobile phones and tablets. Figure 2 shows some examples.

Figure 2: Joule Work mobile app, examples of Conversations and Spaces. Alt Text: two pairs of iPhone images. On the left Joule Work Conversations, showing a start and a screen listing some return orders and a user prompt. On the right a screen with text “Create a new space” and a user input “Create a space to sow my open customer returns for BikePro”, and a screen showing the resulting space with a chart, some cards with recommended tasks, and a return order analysis.

Find out more here. 

  • Joule Work mobile app is here!

What are the benefits for businesses and their users?

Businesses expect that the new AI/agentic, intent-driven way of working that is taking hold across the industry is ushering in a new era of user interaction and user experience, that will bring them gains in productivity and data quality, along with better business insights and outcomes, while saving training costs and reducing user-support costs.

Joule Work from SAP has an ingrained understanding of the customer’s business processes: hence it is best placed to deliver on the above promise. It can provide experiences that bring together the right data and services from across our applications to guide users from initial intent to a successful outcome.

It helps users get their work done faster, more easily, and with better outcomes, by:

  • Providing one place to get their work done, so they don’t have to navigate to many different applications and products, and don’t have to navigate through layers of application UIs, thus reducing the number of clicks and interactions significantly.
  • Allowing users to state their intent in natural language with a conversational interface, then triggering the relevant actions to achieve their intent, and showing the outcome(s) in a personalized way, tailored to the user’s needs.

Which target groups of users and use cases is Joule Work meant for?

Business and technical users. The goal is to help all our customer’s SAP users get their work done more easily, focusing on outcomes, so it is not limited to specific target groups.

The Joule Work Develop experience is provided by Joule Studio. Find out more about the new version of Joule Studio in this news article:

  • Announcing New Joule Studio for Enterprise Scale Agentic Development.

What does this mean for existing UIs and SAP Fiori apps?

Existing SAP products and applications built with SAP’s UI technology stack, as well as other apps in our portfolio, will continue to progress with their roadmap and planned product strategy. Joule Work has no impact on existing UIs, instead it complements them. Customers will continue to get the benefits from existing applications and their investment is protected.

Joule Work provides an additional option for users to interact with the system. Wherever they find that it helps them get tasks done more easily and/or with better outcomes, they are likely to do those tasks with Joule Work. Wherever they feel that their familiar, pre-defined applications are better suited to a task, they will continue using them.

This means that SAP delivers two kinds of experiences:

  • Application experiences (from SAP Cloud ERP, SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Analytics Cloud, etc.): SAP and customer-built transactional and analytical application experiences.
  • Engagement layer experiences (Joule Work experiences): AI-native, agentic, and generative experiences that help users interact with and orchestrate work across those applications.

Our users are used to application UIs and pre-defined navigation paths, how to manage the transition?

The existing product home pages and cross-product entry points such as SAP Build Work Zone and Joule Work mobile app (formerly called SAP Mobile Start) continue to be available for providing pre-defined as well as customer-defined pages and navigation menus. Similarly, the existing applications continue to be available, with their application experiences.

Users working in the web browser can open Joule Work from Joule and hence start getting familiar with the new way of working. Since the new experience co-exists with the current experience it will be easy for users to adapt and adopt. We expect the transition to be user-led, in that they will choose whichever experience helps them get specific tasks done more easily and/or with better outcomes.

If users are already familiar with popular consumer applications such as ChatGPT, Google Gemini or Claude Sonnet, then they will be able to build on this to leverage the next generation user experience provided by Joule Work, with the new ways of interacting.

Does it complement or does it replace existing solutions such as SAP Start, SAP Build Work Zone, and SAP Mobile Start?

It complements them. The existing solutions allow customer administrators to pre-define layouts, content and navigation options, which helps users access applications and information using familiar pages, menus and links. It makes it easy for them to understand the site and feel comfortable using it in their daily work. This is something that benefits all users but is especially helpful for users new to the system to get started quickly and get familiar with their work environment.

SAP Mobile Start has been rebranded to Joule Work mobile app, accompanied by a new app icon and an updated user experience. For customers previously accessing Joule within SAP Mobile Start, the Joule entry point has transitioned from the diamond in the header bar to a full-screen interface to be accessed via the tab bar. All core functionality and features remain unchanged, ensuring continuity for both customers and end users with no disruption to existing workflows. In addition to the core functionality, it provides the Joule Work engagement layer experience for mobile devices. Find out more here:

  • SAP Mobile Start - Rebranding of the app, enhanced Joule and improved single sign-on functionality.

For SAP Cloud ERP Private: would we need to go to SAP Fiori (implement SAP Fiori launchpad), or can we skip it and go to Joule Work directly?

You would need to implement the SAP Fiori launchpad for Joule Work to work with SAP Cloud ERP Private: they use the navigation and authorization information defined by customers in the SAP Fiori launchpad to access the system. Also, Joule only runs in the browser on desktops and is launched from the launchpad. Similarly, if users navigate from Joule Work to applications, this navigation will be to browser-based applications running in the launchpad.

How can partners and customers extend standard SAP functionality for Joule Work – as a key user or as a developer?

The established programming model for creating extensions continues to be valid. Joule Work accesses OData services and their meta-data (amongst others) and hence will also access extensions or custom OData services that have been developed according to the SAP BTP Developer’s Guide.

Using Joule Studio or SAP Build is the way to build extensions and agents, for Joule Work as well as for pre-defined applications.

If you are building new applications, follow the SAP BTP Developer’s Guide, and use Joule Studio or SAP Build. You can build agents on top of the APIs you create, and Joule Work will access the OData services and meta-data of your new applications. The Fiori apps you create using SAPUI5 / SAP Fiori elements will allow users to use your new functionality using apps with pre-defined UIs, an experience that they are familiar with. Also, users can use these UIs to confirm that what was done by Joule Work is indeed correct and hence build trust in it.

What is available today (Sapphire 2026)

As described above, this first version of Joule Work provides Conversations, Spaces, and Develop, with the Joule Work Develop experience being provided by Joule Studio. The Sapphire scope:

  • Supports integration to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and SAP SuccessFactors.
  • Supports exactly one SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, you cannot connect to more than one.
  • Does not support document grounding in Joule.

How can customers get Joule Work, and what are the pre-requisites?

If you are interested, you can sign up to the limited Early Adopter Care (EAC) program. The pre-requisites for this at the time of writing are as follows:

  • Joule needs to be SAP-managed.
    • New SAP-managed Joule instances are supported
    • Existing customers with customer-managed Joule would need to transition to SAP-managed Joule.
  • Available only in specific regions: USA and EU.

Final words

I hope you are as excited as I am about the huge potential these Joule Work experiences have for helping our users get their work done faster and more easily, with better outcomes. It will be interesting to observe how people in various roles adopt this new way of working, and how fast they adopt: for which tasks do users prefer to use Joule Work, for which ones do they continue to prefer application experiences – and why?

If you want to dig a bit deeper, have a look at how we are designing these new experiences, and see how we are evolving the SAP Design System for AI-driven UX:

  • Designing the Autonomous Enterprise.
  • The Compositional Design System.
  • AI and Design.

If you want an overview of interesting UX shown at Sapphire in Orlando this week, have a look at  Peter Spielvogel's nice summary:

  • Sapphire 2026. The future of UX is here. Are you ready for an AI -powered world?

entry.title: From Static Icon to Talking Tile: Adding a Dynamic Fiori Tile for Locked SAP Users
entry.content:
In my previous blog, I described how I built a mobile-friendly Fiori app for basic SAP user administration — lock user, unlock user, reset password, and change validity date.

The idea was simple: if you are on call, travelling, or supporting users across time zones, you do not always want to open SAP GUI from a laptop just to unlock one user. A small Fiori app on a mobile phone can be enough.

And then, of course, once the app was working, the next question appeared:

“Can the tile tell me something before I even open the app?”

At first, my Launchpad tile was static. It had a nice unlock icon, a title, and it opened the app. Technically correct. Visually acceptable. But operationally… it was silent.

I wanted the tile to say:

Unlock User
3 / 130
Locked users

That way, before opening the app, I already know whether there is anything to check.

This blog is about how I converted a static Fiori tile into a dynamic one using an OData endpoint.

 

 Figure 1. Shows Dynamic Tile used in Unlock User Fiori App

The small problem that started it

The application itself already had the logic to search SAP users, display locked users, unlock them, change validity dates, and so on.

But the tile did not know anything.

It was like having a restaurant sign that only says “Kitchen”, but never tells you if there are three orders waiting inside.

So the goal was:

Show number of locked SAP users / total SAP users directly on the Fiori Launchpad tile.

For example:

3 / 130
Locked users

And I also wanted some simple color logic:

Locked users Tile status

0Positive / green
1 - 5Critical / orange
6+Negative / red

High-level idea

A dynamic tile needs a backend service.

The tile calls an OData URL. The OData service returns a JSON response with values such as:

{
  "d": {
    "title": "Unlock User",
    "number": "3",
    "numberUnit": "/ 130",
    "info": "Locked users",
    "numberState": "Critical",
    "infoState": "Critical"
  }
}

SAP Fiori Launchpad then uses this response to display the dynamic number and status on the tile. SAP’s documentation describes dynamic app launcher tiles as tiles whose displayed information can be pulled from a backend system via OData, and the OData structure contains the properties used by the tile. (SAP Help Portal)

Step 1: Create a new OData entity for the tile

In my Gateway project, I added a new entity type.

Example:

Entity Type: TileInfo
Entity Set : TileInfoSet
Key        : id

The properties I used:

Property Type Purpose

idStringKey
titleStringTile title
subtitleStringOptional subtitle
iconStringSAP icon
infoStringFooter text
infoStateStringFooter status
numberDecimal/String-compatibleMain KPI number
numberDigitsInt32Decimal digits
numberFactorStringOptional factor
numberStateStringKPI status/color
numberUnitStringUnit text, in my case / 130
stateArrowStringOptional arrow indicator

After defining the entity, I generated the runtime objects in SEGW.

Step 2: Implement TILEINFOSET_GET_ENTITY

This is the ABAP method that feeds the tile.

The logic is intentionally simple:

  1. Count all users in USR02

  2. Count locked users where UFLAG > 0

  3. Return the locked number as number

  4. Return total users as numberUnit

  5. Set the color/status depending on thresholds

Here is the full method:

METHOD tileinfoset_get_entity.

  DATA lv_total  TYPE i.
  DATA lv_locked TYPE i.

  CLEAR er_entity.

  SELECT COUNT( * )
    FROM usr02
    INTO @lv_total.

  SELECT COUNT( * )
    FROM usr02
    WHERE uflag > 0
    INTO @lv_locked.

  er_entity-id           = 'UNLOCK'.
  er_entity-title        = 'Unlock User'.
  er_entity-subtitle     = ''.
  er_entity-icon         = 'sap-icon://unlocked'.
  er_entity-info         = 'Locked users'.
  er_entity-number       = lv_locked.
  er_entity-numberunit   = |/ { lv_total }|.
  er_entity-numberdigits = 0.
  er_entity-numberfactor = ''.
  er_entity-statearrow   = 'None'.

  IF lv_locked = 0.

    er_entity-numberstate = 'Positive'.
    er_entity-infostate   = 'Positive'.

  ELSEIF lv_locked <= 5.

    er_entity-numberstate = 'Critical'.
    er_entity-infostate   = 'Critical'.

  ELSE.

    er_entity-numberstate = 'Negative'.
    er_entity-infostate   = 'Negative'.

  ENDIF.

ENDMETHOD.

That is the whole “brain” behind the tile.

No magic. Just one small OData endpoint that answers one very useful question:

“How many users are locked right now?”

Step 3: Test the OData service

Before touching Launchpad configuration, I tested the service in /IWFND/GW_CLIENT.

Request:

/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZTEST5_SRV/TileInfoSet('UNLOCK')?$format=json

Expected response:

{
  "d": {
    "id": "UNLOCK",
    "title": "Unlock User",
    "subtitle": "",
    "icon": "sap-icon://unlocked",
    "info": "Locked users",
    "infoState": "Critical",
    "number": "3",
    "numberDigits": 0,
    "numberFactor": "",
    "numberState": "Critical",
    "numberUnit": "/ 130",
    "stateArrow": "None"
  }
}

Once I got HTTP 200 OK, I knew the backend part was fine.

At this point, the tile still did not show anything dynamic. That is normal. The backend was ready, but Launchpad still had to be told where to read the data from.

Step 4: Configure the tile as Dynamic in Fiori Launchpad App Manager

In my case I used:

/UI2/FLPAM

I opened my technical catalog and selected the app descriptor item for my app.

Then, in the Tiles tab, I changed or verified the tile configuration:

Field Value

Tile TypeDynamic
TitleUnlock User
Tile InformationLocked users
Tile Iconsap-icon://unlocked
Refresh Interval300
Service URL/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZTEST5_SRV/TileInfoSet('UNLOCK')?$format=json

The refresh interval defines how often the tile should refresh its dynamic data. SAP documentation for dynamic tiles also shows configuration through Launchpad tooling, including use of an OData service for real-time information display. (SAP Help Portal)

In some systems, if the URL with quotes causes problems, the encoded version can be used:

/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZTEST5_SRV/TileInfoSet(%27UNLOCK%27)?$format=json

Step 5: Optional manifest configuration

If the app descriptor is maintained through the UI5 app manifest, the dynamic tile can also be described with an indicatorDataSource.

Example:

"indicatorDataSource": {
  "dataSource": "mainService",
  "path": "TileInfoSet('UNLOCK')",
  "refresh": 300
}

This tells the Launchpad which OData model and path to use for the tile indicator.

In my case, I still verified the final configuration in FLPAM, because sometimes the Launchpad catalog configuration is the place where the tile actually lives.

Step 6: Clear cache, because of course

This is SAP. The code can be perfect and still look wrong until the right cache is cleared.

The usual suspects:

/UI2/INVALIDATE_GLOBAL_CACHES
/UI2/INVALIDATE_CLIENT_CACHES
/IWFND/CACHE_CLEANUP
/IWBEP/CACHE_CLEANUP

Then I removed the old static tile from My Home and added the app again from the App Finder.

After a browser hard refresh, the tile finally started talking.

The result

Instead of a static tile with only an icon, I now had:

Unlock User

3 / 130
Locked users

And because numberState was returned as Critical, the tile displayed the number in orange.

Exactly what I wanted.

Not a dashboard. Not a full monitoring solution. Just a small piece of useful information directly where the user starts working.

Lessons learned

1. A dynamic tile is not a custom tile

This is an important distinction.

I did not need to build a custom SAPUI5 tile. A standard Dynamic App Launcher Tile was enough.

That means less frontend code, less maintenance, and less trouble.

2. The backend response format matters

The tile expects specific property names such as:

number
numberUnit
info
numberState
infoState

If the OData service works technically but the fields are wrong, the Launchpad will not display what you expect.

3. Always test in Gateway Client first

If the OData endpoint does not return the correct JSON in /IWFND/GW_CLIENT, there is no point debugging the Launchpad tile.

First make the service work. Then configure FLP.

4. Cache is part of the development process

I would love to say the tile changed immediately.

It did not.

Clear cache, remove the old tile, add it again, refresh the browser — and then judge the result.

5. Small UX improvements matter

This change does not unlock users faster by itself.

But it tells the support person whether there is anything to look at before opening the app.

That is the kind of small improvement that makes a custom Fiori app feel more useful.

Final thought

In the first version of my app, the tile was just a door.

Now it is a door with a small sign on it:

3 users are waiting.

And sometimes that is exactly what a support user needs.

Not another report.
Not another dashboard.
Just the right number, in the right place, before the first click.

entry.title: RAP - Step-by-Step Guide to Creating an Overview Page Dashboard with Fiori Elements
entry.content:
Overview page is generally used for grouping Embedded Analytics upon different dimension which flows towards individual entities. But, Have you ever wondered on creating overview page with RAP on Backend. This blog will explain on the same in detail. 

Overview for creating Overview page - Dashboard:

We are going with complete end-end cloud ready approach and for the same I've used BTP Cloud foundry Environment for this Dashboard creation. Below are the sequential steps to create overview page Dashboard. 

Backend:

1. Set up BTP Cloud foundry Environment and enable it in Eclipse ADT.

2. Create Individual Entities using RAP Custom Entity/CDS View. 

3. Create Filter Entity which will be enabled as common live Filter

4. Create Business logic class for these Custom Entities.

Front End:

1. Create New Project with Overview Page Template

2. Link the Entities in BAS and create different cards as per the requirement.

3. Dry run the app to debug and check backend and front end is interlinked and fiori element is configured properly. 

Below Flow chart explains the complete process precisely.

Now let's start the Development. 

 Set up BTP Cloud foundry Environment and enable it in Eclipse ADT

1. Login to your BTP Cockpit as below. 

2. Click Boosters from the left-hand navigation pane of your Global Account view and select prepare an Account for ABAP Trail.

3. The system automatically assigns roles, establishes the Cloud Foundry runtime, builds a dev space, and spins up the ABAP service instance.Once complete, skip down to the Generate a Service Key. Download the service key once the process is completed and store it locally.

4. Now navigate to SubAccount, Instances and Subscriptions and locate the ABAP instance. Right click on 3 dots to create service key.

5. Once service key is created, click on it and navigate to the JSON that it shows. Now, copy the JSON and navigate to Eclipse.

6. Now in Eclipse create new project and enter the JSON that has been copied from BTP Cockpit. This JSON will create the cloud project in Eclipse. 

Create Individual Entities using RAP Custom Entity/CDS View
